speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three of an interview with Mr. Kurt Moses on January 16, 1996, in Pompano Beach, Florida.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] was an experience that I find very difficult to describe, that human beings exist who are capable of doing things, what this man did. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He-- he got pleasure out of killing people, the Jews, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he always-- it was more of a challenge to him to pick the big and the tall ones. And there wasn't a day that I remember that he wasn't drunk. We had to get out in the middle of the night and to stand on the Appellplatz, which was the center of the camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We had to-- in the wintertime, I remember we had to stand naked there. We had to crawl in the snow, and the winters are severe there. Most of the time, he did this-- what he called gymnastics after you came in from a day's work, from 10, 12 hours in the factory. The camp itself-- we are-- we had about 2,000 in these little labor camps, and they were just barracks. Didn't get a chance to wash yourself, or we didn't have any underwear. There weren't any showers. If you're lucky, there was a faucet, and if you had a chance to use the faucet to wash yourself off a little bit-- looking back, I-- I never had any underwear when we were there. Food was terrible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Sleeping quarters-- we had a barrack, and there were three-tier wooden shelves like, and that's where you lied on. And when you woke up in the morning, and you looked around, and there were always one or so that didn't make the night, and you wondered, maybe they're better off than you are. You woke up and-- realizing where you are, what you have to face again, and you reach the stage where you start thinking about probably better off not being there and face all this again. And the suicide rate was-- was pretty high, and it was easy to do because the camp was surrounded with high-voltage wire, and all you had to do is go and touch the wire. And of course, the first thing every morning, you saw that they had a special commando of prisoners who had that cart, and they used to go out the camp first and pick up all the bodies that were lying there of prisoners who went there during the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: To escape, I think from my experience, was an impossibility in those camps. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, even if you would get out of the camp, where would you go to? I mean, here you are, looking almost like a skeleton with a striped suit in a country where you can't speak the language. And don't forget, there were days where Germany were-- was winning the war, and people were not too unfriendly against the German regime because they benefited a lot by what they did to us.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember when-- the short distance that we had to march from the camp to the factory. Germany was winning the war. People stood there and-- what they yelled at us, and the children were allowed to kick us and throw stones at us, and-- and-- and you felt like, you know, you weren't human anymore. You're a part of the-- you were treated like an animal. And I think I learned that-- that killing a human being really isn't the biggest crime. It's to degrade him, to-- to dehumanize him, and I think that's what-- what we were. People sometimes asked you question about your thinking. You-- you weren't able. You weren't in a state to think like normal human beings. You were-- that's it. I remember when we marched to and back-- back and forth to the factory. The road you had to march on, people, I guess, didn't have a sewer system. They had a gutter, and the garbage was thrown in the gutter. And then, when the rain came, it washed away the thing. Now, the trick was, when you had to march-- we always had to line up in five-- to be on the outside so that you're close to the gutter so when you could spot something that was edible that you took a quick [? grab ?] and-- and then have something to eat. If you were caught doing this by the guards, you were punished. Your number was written down, and then, once a week or so, they had payday, what they called. You had to line up, and the numbers were called. And then you got your punishment for what you did, depending on how many lashes. Usually, when you get over the 30 mark, you didn't get up anymore. That was the end of it. But it didn't mean anything to you because your reactions-- you weren't thinking of what can happen to you if you get caught. All was on your mind was there was something to eat, and you had to go for it. And that's how you lived, day in and day out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Germans were always, how should I say, very culturally thinking. You had all these slogans and all these-- these poems that they had written in the barracks and so forth. And our little camp, we had a band. We had a band. It consisted of five or six musicians. And every morning when we left for work, the men stood at the exit and played the same march every day, and the SS men in charge stood on the other side, and he counted how many rows of five were leaving the camp. And the same thing happened when you came back, and that count had to be exact. If there was one missing, then you stood there for hours and hours. And I remember sometimes we had an accident in a factories, and one of our men got killed there by having the cart run over him. We had to carry this man back in camp, and he had to be counted to make sure that the amount was correct. And I remember an incident when we marched out in the morning. And this is the way they ran it. They had a prisoner. I remember him well. He was a Czech. He was from Czechoslovakia, and they had his body displayed at the exit, naked, with bullet wounds-- wounds, and they had a sign next to it saying, this man tried to escape, and this is the price he had to pay. And then, when we came to this point, you all had to turn all our heads to make sure that we were looking at this particular portrait. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] other incidents-- they just fabricated things. Of course, we weren't allowed to have any material things. And I remember one time, the guard said they had caught a man who had-- still had a gold ring that he had kept, and his wedding band or whatever, and he hadn't given that when he was supposed to. And he-- this man in front of us, he just beat him to death. So I mean, I'm sure this man never had anything, but this-- these were the situations. These were the lessons that they were showing us. This is what's happening to you if you don't obey by what we tell you to do. And the result, of course, was that you did what you had to do, and you were just hoping and-- there were times where you really didn't think you were going to make it, and I think the fortunate thing, the lucky thing, was that we really-- the five of us kept together, and we were able to help each other and-- and how shall I say, give each other-- come on. You know, you've kept up till now. It's not going to last that much longer. Someday, something is gonna happen. You know. After all, the world can't just stand by all the time. But Gleiwitz was a very, very sad, very bad period, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think when the time came that we had to leave Gleiwitz, I don't think anybody really was sad about it. No matter what the outcome would be, no matter where we would go, you had seen the worse-- you know, only  can get better. And sure enough, one morning when we came to the factory-- and this was very unusual. It never happened before. Instead of one ration of bread, we got two rations of bread, and something was wrong. Something didn't-- didn't click right. And there were rumors they were demantling machinery already in the factory before, and they were shipping it back into Germany. But of course, we-- you have no contact with the outside world. You don't know what's happening. The war, invade-- we didn't know any of those things. And occasionally, we had seen a Russian plane flying, but that wasn't anything to, you know, make a roof over their cell, but the things started when they gradually exchanged the SS guards. More of the younger people, more of the younger guards, were replaced with older guards, men in their 60s or 50s, whatever it was. And the bad thing, of course, was that, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, looking back, you think, OK, you take a young German who grew up in the Hitler Jugend who was trained, and educated, and-- and taught all the Nazi theories. He probably might not have known better. But to take a man who is in his 60s and who has a family and to do the same thing that this young person did-- he did, and they'd try to keep up and make sure that they did just as good a job with-- than the younger ones did, and they did. And I sometimes wonder, looking back-- I wish I would have met somebody who would have shown a little compassion, understanding, would have given an extra piece of bread or something like that so at least at this time I could say, well, they weren't all bad. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Didn't occur. Didn't happen. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] I wish sometime it would. So then, we were lined up after we had the second piece of bread, and we were told that the factory is gonna be closed, and they're going to move us into Germany. We had to go to the railroad station. We would be transferred to a-- a German factory into Germany. We went. Train never arrived. They said the railroad tracks were damaged or bombed, and the train never came. I don't know how long we were there waiting.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, they decided we can't go back. We should march into Germany as far as we get. And they had combined the other two Gleiwitz camps with us, so there were six-- about 6,000 of us. And then, we started to march, and this was in January '45. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Winters in that part of the country are very severe, and this was a lot of snow, a lot of ice, a lot of cold. And we just marched, and you came to the point where you couldn't march anymore. Those who were unable to continue, they were just shot alongside the road and just left there. I think the third day we marched, and during the night, we were put up in a-- it used to be a French prisoner of war camp, which had been vacated-- was empty. And they put us into those barracks just for the night. And the next morning, we were supposed to go on. And that was the time where we really-- the five of us, we sat there and said look, we just can't. Just-- you couldn't. You couldn't-- physically, you couldn't do it, and we saw what happened to the ones that-- on the way, so we decided we're not gonna go. We're gonna stay here. We try to hide, and they-- all that may happen, happens. That's the the end of it. And the next morning, early, they called, and everybody had to get out, and they started counting again. But the guards were very nervous, and everything had to go schnell, schnell, very quick, quick. And they realized that not all prisoners were out there, and we hid under the bunks in the barracks. So if we are found, we are found. It's over. But apparently, they didn't want to take the time to look for them. They just put up a few machine guns, and they just mowed through the barracks. And luckily, we weren't hit, and we were lying there. I don't know for how long. And then, we found out the noise was gone, and we looked, and they had marched, and we were in the barracks. And I think we were there until the next day, and all of a sudden, this Russian tank-- OK. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what day that was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think-- I think it was January 17, something like that. I remember this-- this tank, and this-- this thing opened up, and this-- this guy stuck his-- his head out, and he just looked at us. And then he closed it again and left. Here we were, but at least then we knew that the Germans weren't there, that the Russians were there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There was a-- you know, we were in between the fire. For all the night, you could hear it, the shooting, and then all this, back and forth. We were in between, but then the Germans apparently retreated, and the Russians were there. And this was in a forest. You didn't see any houses or any other people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So here we are now, lying in this barrack. And of course, the first thing on your mind is food. How can we get food? And we went into-- went to this kitchen of this [NON-ENGLISH], this prisoner of war camp, but there wasn't much left. Was some-- whatever there was, we-- right away, we-- we ate. And unfortunately, I remember there was one bottle, and it looked like grape juice, and one of our five took the bottle and then drank it, and he died. [PAUSES FOR 16 SECONDS] Then what's the next step? Where you gonna go? What are you gonna do? You're in a strange country. You can't communicate with anybody. This was the worst. You picked up a few words. And here, you're in a striped-- you know, in a nothing. What are you going to do? So of course, first we started-- when we walked out of the area, we went-- there were a lot of farms there, and we start begging for food. We has to. And I had the feeling that when they saw us, a lot of them were afraid not to say no. No, sure, sure. You know. You could see by the reaction of-- of other people, you know, that we-- we didn't do this to you. You know. Sure. What do you want? And they're very helpful and very-- but they wanted to get rid of us as soon as possible. Whatever we wanted, they gave us, but please leave, you know, which was fine. So the first thing, of course, we did was to make sure that we had enough to eat and that we-- we we went to-- we went back to Gleiwitz, which was a city. Of course, it's a pretty big city. And they had set up like Red Cross agencies and so forth, and we went there, and they gave us-- we got rid of our striped clothes. We got underwear. We got clothes. They gave us food to eat and so forth.

speaker: interviewee
text: And don't forget, the war wasn't over until May '45. This is January, so we weren't able to go home yet. So we had to stay around, which-- we didn't know when the war was to be over. And the first thing we did when we felt a little bit more strength, we went back to Auschwitz, and although we knew what had happened at the selection, but you still-- you know, you never give up hope. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Yep.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to Auschwitz, what did you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we wanted to see who we know. What about Father? Maybe some miracle happened, that he was still survive. So we looked. We stayed in Auschwitz. I went to-- we went to city hall, and I figure we didn't have any IDs. We didn't have any papers. Nobody knew who we were, what my name is, and so-- I mean, here years have passed. I-- I couldn't even-- I tried a pencil. I couldn't even write anymore. It was just, you know, things that you take for granted. You just-- it just isn't there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to city hall, and we told them that we're from Holland, and we want to go back-- to give us some idea. The problem was that the Russians used to arrest the Germans on the streets and send them to Russia, to Siberia to work, and we didn't look quite as well as they did, but we were afraid, since we couldn't communicate them, that they took us maybe for different as what we were. So we-- city hall in Auschwitz gave us a certificate in different languages that we had been prisoners there and that we are back on our-- on our way back home, and they should cooperate helping us. And I still have that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, of course, we went back to-- we went to Krakow. We stayed behind the Russian lines. We felt as long as they keep on moving forward, then we're right behind them. And we were in Krakow, which is a big city. We stayed in Krakow for a while. And then when they-- when they took Czechoslovakia, I wanted to go back to Theresienstadt because my mother and sister were last-- had last been in Theresienstadt. So finally, we reached that period, and we got to Theresienstadt. However, Theresienstadt-- when we got to the camp, they had a typhus epidemic. The camp was closed. They wouldn't allow anybody in. And I talked to somebody in the gate, and I said, would you be able to find out if-- hey, they came back. And he said yes, they're both alive. They're both there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then, we waited till nighttime, and we climbed over the fence. And sure enough, I met my sister. And then they had like a little hospital then. And she told me my mother had typhus, and she was in the hospital there at the time. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] This is-- this is quite difficult. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] You have to understand that [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my family was very close, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very close family. My parents were-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] we never-- we grew up-- never heard an argument or a fight, or a disagreement, and [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] it's-- it's difficult for me. It's hard to describe, but I knew my mother would never survive if she knew that my father wasn't there. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] [DRINKS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And when she saw me, of course the first thing was, where's Dad? And I told her. I said, he went home already. I said, he's waiting for you.
